title: Solving 2D Black Scholes Equation via Hermitian Block Embedding and Generalised Quantum Signal Processing
authors: James W. Greenwell, Jingbo Wang, Des Hill
abstract: The Black Scholes equation provides a fundamental model for the no arbitrage pricing of financial derivatives. After finite difference discretisation, the pricing problem can be formulated as a finite dimensional linear algebra problem involving the inverse of a non Hermitian time step matrix. Recent advances in quantum linear algebra algorithms, particularly the generalised quantum signal processing (GQSP)algorithm, enable matrix functions to be implemented through polynomial transformations of a suitable unitary or Hermitian form. In this paper, we develop a Hermitian block embedding method that enables GQSP to be applied to the two dimensional Black Scholes equation. Numerical simulations for two asset European call options are performed to evaluate the proposed approach. GQSP based solutions are benchmarked against the classical polynomial approximation with backward Euler finite difference method, showing close agreement. This indicates that the Hermitian block embedding construction accurately captures the dynamics of the original non Hermitian operator. These results demonstrate the feasibility of combining Hermitian block embeddings with GQSP for multidimensional Black Scholes problems and provide a proof of principle for applying modern quantum linear algebra techniques to option pricing.
summary: This paper adapts quantum linear algebra techniques, specifically GQSP, to solve the complex 2D Black-Scholes equation. It overcomes the challenge of non-Hermitian operators by developing a Hermitian block-embedding method. The results prove the feasibility of using this quantum approach for multidimensional option pricing, benchmarking against classical methods.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of solving the two-dimensional Black-Scholes equation, which models no-arbitrage option pricing, by implementing the required matrix function inversion using quantum algorithms.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrate that a Hermitian block-embedding method allows the application of Generalised Quantum Signal Processing (GQSP) to the 2D Black-Scholes problem, yielding results that closely match classical benchmarks.
method: The core method involves embedding the non-Hermitian time-step matrix into a larger Hermitian block matrix, which then allows the use of GQSP to approximate the inverse matrix function via an odd polynomial transformation.
model_or_system: The model is the two-dimensional Black-Scholes equation for pricing two-asset European options. After finite-difference discretization, this results in a linear algebra problem requiring the inversion of a non-Hermitian time-step matrix.
key_observables: Option value $V(S_1, S_2, t)$ at different time steps; absolute error surfaces comparing quantum, polynomial, and classical solutions.
important_parameters: Volatility ($\sigma_1, \sigma_2$), correlation ($ho$), time step ($\Delta	au$), and the spectral gap ($\delta$) of the Hermitian embedding.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the inverse action can be accurately captured by an odd polynomial approximation applied to the spectrum of the Hermitian block-embedded matrix.
figures_summary: Figures compare the terminal payoff and absolute error surfaces between the quantum GQSP solution, a classical polynomial approximation, and the standard backward Euler benchmark for the 2D case.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the 1D case success, identifying the failure of simple diagonalization in 2D due to closed loops, and then introducing and validating the Hermitian block-embedding technique to enable GQSP application for the 2D inverse step.
why_interesting: While the application is financial, the underlying mathematical technique—transforming a non-Hermitian operator into a Hermitian one for quantum simulation—is highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum dynamics where Hamiltonian simulation is common.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Polynomial approximation and corresponding absolute error for the two-dimensional inverse step.

Figure 2: Comparison of the two-dimensional terminal payoff, classical backward Euler solu- tion, polynomial approximation and the scaled quantum/GQSP output. The terminal payoff is given by the basket call payoff at maturity, while the classical and quantum surfaces show the result after one backward time step. The close agreement between the scaled quantum output, polynomial approximation and the classical backward Euler solution indicates that the GQSP block-embedding construction reproduces the intended inverse time-step action.

Figure 4, shows a slice of the payoff, by fixing one asset and varying the other, this reveals that the two-dimensional solution is working well for both assets, with different financial parameters, it is clear to see that indeed each asset has been evolved independently.

Figure 3: Absolute error surfaces for the two-dimensional simulation. The left panel shows |Vpoly −VBE|, comparing the polynomial approximation with the same classical benchmark. The right panel shows |VQ −VBE|, comparing the scaled quantum/GQSP output with the classical backward Euler solution.
Figure 4: Two-dimensional Black–Scholes solution slices for fixed S1 and fixed S2.
